exemplar.title: The relationship between the surface temperature and the output voltage of a solar cell

detailedExemplarData.title: The relationship between the surface temperature and the output voltage of a solar cell

subject.title: Physics
subject.description:
**IB Physics** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **space, time and motion; the particulate nature of matter; wave behaviour; fields; and nuclear and quantum physics**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, data analysis, and scientific investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice questions and data-based questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 1 hour 30 minutes | 30% |
| **Paper 2** | Short-answer and extended-response questions | 1 hour 30 minutes | 2 hours 15 minutes | 50% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Individual scientific investigation | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Data tables and graphs are exceptionally clear and precise, including units, uncertainties, averages, error bars, best-fit lines and max/min gradient envelopes.
Uncertainty sources are identified and propagated; instrument uncertainties are stated and integrated into calculated gradients.
Data processing (averaging, regression, gradient/intercept extraction and theory comparison) is carried out appropriately and accurately.
Presentation of processed data supports robust interpretation and reproducibility.
weaknesses: Quantitative propagation details are sometimes abbreviated; intermediate steps for combined uncertainties could be clearer.
Rounding inconsistencies occur in sample calculations, occasionally under-reflecting instrument precision.
Systematic uncertainties (e.g., illuminometer calibration, lamp distance) are noted but not deeply analysed.
Uncertainty bounds for slope and intercept are omitted in the regression equation description.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student communicates data recording and processing with both clarity and precision and performs accurate, appropriate calculations. While a few propagation and systematic-uncertainty details are under-developed, overall the analysis meets the highest band descriptors.

strengths: The research question is explicitly framed in a specific context, linking semiconductor band-gap theory to surface temperature effects on output voltage.
A clear labelled diagram, equipment list, step-by-step method and variables table enable reproducibility with only minor ambiguities.
Relevant variables are identified and justified; the 11-point temperature range with repeats provides sufficient data to answer the question.
weaknesses: Exact lampâcell separation, heating duration and ambient light shielding are omitted, introducing slight ambiguity for replication.
Alignment âby eyeâ is subjective; a fixed measurement protocol would improve consistency.
Illuminance uncertainty is stated with unrealistic precision; a more accurate instrument calibration and uncertainty estimate are needed.
Uncontrolled variables (e.g. dust) are listed without discussion of their potential effect.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The design shows a high level of specificity and context, and the methodology is largely reproducible. Minor omissions in procedural detail and over-precision in uncertainty reporting prevent a perfect score, placing the work firmly in the top-but-one band.

strengths: The conclusion is fully justified by and consistent with the data analysis, explicitly linking the negative trend and quantified gradient to the hypothesis.
Findings are compared to accepted literature values, with discussion of material-dependent temperature coefficients and non-linear effects at extremes.
Discrepancies are explained using percentage error and theoretical context, demonstrating depth of understanding.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion synthesises experimental evidence with theoretical expectations and places the results in a broader scientific context. It meets all criteria for justification and consistency with analysis, warranting the top mark.

strengths: Specific methodological limitations (thermometer interference, contact resistance, wire length, ambient conditions) are identified with discussion of their effects on voltage readings.
Realistic and relevant improvements (contact sensor substitution, thicker wires, environmental monitoring) are proposed with enough detail for implementation.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each limitation is not quantified, leaving ambiguous which weaknesses are most critical.
Proposed improvements lack quantitative explanation (e.g., calibration validation, measurement of residual resistance).
Suggestions for extensions (testing other cell types) are relevant but not supported by measurable parameters or protocol adaptations.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes relevant weaknesses and gives practical improvements, but falls short of fully explaining their relative impact or providing quantitative justification. This places it in the middle of the 3â4 band.

strandSelections.reasoning: The research question is explicitly stated and embedded within a focused context of photovoltaic efficiency, band-gap behaviour and real-world optimisation of solar panels. Background information links semiconductor physics to the specific variable (surface temperature) and performance measure (output voltage), demonstrating a clear, appropriate and specific context rather than a broad or generic one.

Instrument uncertainties are stated; error bars plotted; gradients and intercepts include propagated or estimated uncertainties. Nevertheless, propagation is sometimes approximate (negative sign in âm, assumption that averaging always reduces uncertainty) and systematic sources (lamp distance, illuminometer calibration) are largely overlooked. These omissions prevent full âappropriateâ treatment.

The student identifies relevant variables, explains their significance, and selects an 11-point temperature range with five repeats, yielding sufficient data to address the question. Methodological considerations (instrument precision, controlled light intensity, angle, wavelength) are described but not fully explained in depth (e.g., quantitative justification of illuminance level), so the strand sits comfortably in the âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ band.

Multiple specific weaknesses (IR thermometer interference, contact resistance, wire length, ambient conditions) are described with their likely impact on voltage readings. Relative significance is hinted at but not quantitatively analysed, so the work exceeds simple statements yet falls short of fully explaining comparative impact.

A labelled diagram, equipment list, step-by-step method, and tables of variables are provided, enabling reproduction of the investigation with reasonable confidence. However, some details (e.g., exact lampâcell separation, heating duration, ambient light shielding) are omitted, leaving minor ambiguities that an experienced replicator would need to infer.

Averages, linear regression, max/min gradient envelope, intercept extraction, percentage error and comparison with theory are carried out correctly and coherently. Calculations are largely accurate and logically sequenced, demonstrating appropriate and accurate processing suitable for the investigation.

For each stated limitation a realistic, relevant improvement is provided (e.g., thermocouple substitution, thicker wires, environmental monitoring). The suggestions are described with enough detail to implement, but the explanations of how each improvement would quantitatively enhance reliability remain under-developed, placing the work in the âdescribedâ rather than âexplainedâ band.

Raw and processed data tables include units, uncertainties and averages; the graph is fully labelled with error bars and best-fit plus max/min gradients. These features show both clarity (readable, well-organised) and precision (correct units, significant figures largely consistent), justifying placement in the top band, albeit with a minor deduction for occasional rounding lapses.

The student explicitly compares findings to literature values (0.5 % / Â°C) and discusses material-dependent temperature coefficients, band-gap effects and non-linear behaviour at extremes, providing a relevant, reasoned scientific context that supports and extends the discussion.

The conclusion directly synthesises experimental evidence (negative linear trend, quantified gradient) with the stated hypothesis and discusses discrepancies using calculated percentage error. It is fully consistent with the analysis and justifies claims within the investigated temperature range.

annotations.comment: The research question is explicitly framed around surface temperatureâs effect on output voltage, integrating semiconductor band-gap theory with photovoltaic efficiency. This specific and contextual focus aligns well with the investigationsâ aims.

Background section provides solid physics but omits inâtext citations for the band-gap discussion. Adding citations (e.g., semiconductor textbooks or review articles) would strengthen the scientific foundation and trace key equations.

The preliminary trials section notes optimization steps but does not include any representative data or outcomes. Including a summary table of trial results would verify setup choices and inform the main method.

Aligning the lamp and cell âby eyeâ introduces subjectivity. Specify a fixed distance and use measurement tools (e.g., ruler or laser pointer) to ensure reproducible alignment and consistent irradiance.

Method step 10 describes repeating trials but omits the exact number of repeats for each temperature. Explicitly state âfive repeats per temperatureâ here to align with the dataâcollection plan.

Controlled variables table lists illuminance as 35.76 Â±0.0005 lumens, which is unrealistically precise given typical instrument accuracy. Reassess and state a realistic uncertainty or calibration procedure for the illuminometer.

The uncontrolled variables table mentions âdust and dirtâ but leaves its significance blank. Discuss how accumulated contaminants could attenuate irradiance or alter cell responsivity to indicate potential data bias.
Raw and processed data tables are clearly presented with units, uncertainties, and averages. The organization facilitates reproducibility and supports clear progression to graphical analysis.

The explanation that averaging reduces uncertainty lacks quantitative support. A brief calculation of standard error (Ï/ân) would clarify how random errors combine and demonstrate understanding of uncertainty propagation.

Sample calculation rounds 1.184 to 1.18; significant figures should reflect instrument precision (Â±0.005 V). Explicitly state rounding rules or retain three significant figures where supported by uncertainty.

Graph includes error bars, best-fit line, and max/min gradient envelopes, demonstrating both clarity and precision in data presentation. This comprehensive communication supports robust trend interpretation.

Description of best-fit equation omits uncertainty bounds on gradient and intercept. Including these uncertainties would strengthen the quantitative analysis and reflect the dataâs precision limits.

Propagation of percentage uncertainty in combined variables appears formulaic but lacks a detailed breakdown. Display intermediate %Îm and %ÎVâ contributions before summation to clarify the procedure.
The negative sign in the uncertainty of the slope (Îm) is misleading; absolute values should be used when expressing uncertainty. Correcting this would align with proper uncertainty conventions.

The conclusion synthesizes experimental evidence with theoretical expectations and acknowledges discrepancies, demonstrating a consistent and justified interpretation of results within the investigated range.

Scientific context comparison is thorough; linking the measured rate coefficient to literature values and discussing material-dependent deviations adds depth and supports the conclusionâs broader relevance.

Environmental factors are identified as a key limitation, but their relative impact on heat loss and voltage drift isnât quantified. Estimating the magnitude of temperature fluctuation would clarify its significance.

The suggestion to switch to a contact-based sensor is appropriate, but it lacks discussion of how sensor response time or calibration would be validated. Including these details would support practical implementation.

Internal resistance of the solar cell is correctly identified as a source of error. To explain its relative impact, compare voltage drop at different current loads to quantify how much this contributes versus temperature effects.

Use of short, thick wires to minimize resistance is practical. Consider measuring the residual wire resistance and applying a correction factor to demonstrate how much this improvement would reduce voltage error.

The proposed extension to test perovskite, CdTe, and CIGS cells is relevant. Strengthen this by specifying measurable parameters (e.g., temperature coefficient ranges) and outlining how the same protocol would be adapted.

rubricData.rubricSpecificInfo: âThe Physics Internal Assessment (IA) is a critical component of the IB Diploma Programme, providing students with the opportunity to design and conduct independent investigations into physics phenomena. This assessment emphasizes the development of practical skills, analytical thinking, and a deep understanding of scientific methodologies. By engaging in the IA, students not only apply theoretical knowledge to real-world scenarios but also cultivate competencies in research design, data analysis, and critical evaluation, all of which are essential for academic and professional success in scientific fields. â
